State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statement of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662     322-5660      322-3901          322-6441                    322-6444

March 16, 1983


Clayton Roche
Deputy Attorney General
350 McAllister St., Room 6000
San Francisco, CA  94102

     Re: Your Opinion No. 82-1207
          Our No. A-83-071

Dear Mr. Roche:

     Thank you for forwarding Mr. Lamoree's opinion request to
this office. Most of the matters covered by the opinion request
are outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. However, I
would like to point out, in connection with Mr. Lamoree's
analysis of the Political Reform Act (pages 5-6 of his letter),
that his salary as a fire chief is a salary from a local government agency and thus is not "income" within the meaning of the
Political Reform Act. Government Code Section 82030(b)(2). 1/
As a result, the fire district is not a "source of income" to him,
and he would not have to disqualify himself, pursuant to Sections
37100 and 37103, from making decisions as a county supervisor
which would affect the fire district or which would affect his
own salary as an employee of the district. 2 Cal. Admin. Code
Section 18700(d)(3) and (f)(2) (a copy of 18700 is included for
your convenience).

     Thank you for giving us an opportunity to comment on this
request.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signed]
                                        /Barbara A. Milman
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

BAM:ms


_____________
     1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code
unless otherwise stated.

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

State of California
DEPARTMENT OF JUSTICE

350 McALLISTER STREET, ROOM 6000
SAN FRANCISCO 94102
(415) 557-2544

MAR 15 8 44 AM '83

(415) 557-1586

March 14, 1983

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

Dear Ms. Milman:

Re: Opinion No. 82-1207

Enclosed is a copy of an opinion request which I have begun
work on but had overlooked forwarding to your office at the
time of receipt for views on the conflict of interest aspects
of this dual office holding.

Accordingly, any views you may wish to submit are hereby
requested.

Very truly yours,

JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP
Attorney General

[illegible signature]

CLAYTON P. ROCHE
Deputy Attorney General

CPR:lv
encls.

OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL
COUNTY COURT HOUSE
FAIRFIELD, CALIFORNIA 94533
(707) 423-6491

CHARLES C. LAMOBEE
COUNTY COUNSEL

THOMAS F. CORDINER
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL
JOHN P. CHAMP
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL
JOHN F. DEAN
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL
DVEN L. GRANT JR.
DEPUTY COUNTY COUNSEL
[illegible]

Honorable Jack R. Winkler
Assistant Attorney General
Chief, Opinion Unit
Office of the Attorney General
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 350
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Mr. Winkler:

I respectfully request the opinion of the Attorney General on
the following questions:

1. DO the positions of fire chief of a county fire protection district and member of a county board of supervisors constitute incompatible offices?

2. From the perspective of the county board of supervisors,
does the position of fire chief present a conflict of
interest arising from employment as opposed to a conflict
arising from the holding of an office?

3. From the perspective of the fire protection district,
does the position of fire chief and county supervisor
present a conflict of interest arising from employment
as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of an
office?

4. If the district fire chief were to waive his salary and
act in a volunteer capacity as fire chief (or district
firefighter) would the analyses be different from that
posed in question (3) above?

5. Is any conflict avoided by the district board becoming
an elective body?

FACTUAL BACKGROUND

The Fire Chief of the Elmira Fire Protection District was
recently elected to the Solano County Board of Supervisors
and will assume office on January 2, 1985.

The Elmira Fire Protection District is organized pursuant
the provisions of the Fire Protection District Law of 1961
(Health and Safety Code §§13801 et seq.) The district is located

Honorable Jack R. Winkler
Page 2
------

entirely within the unincorporated territory of Solano County.
The Board of Supervisors of Solano County is the "supervising
authority" of the district (R&S Code §13306), although the
general powers and duties of the district reside with a five-
person Board of Directors. The Directors are appointed by the
Solano County Board of Supervisors and serve four-year terms,
subject to removal by the Board of Supervisors for cause [R&S
Code §13831(b)].1/

The District Board of Directors has the power to ". . . appoint
and employ necessary employees, and to define their qualifications,
duties and responsibilities,. . . and to provide for the pay
of district employees." The district board has created the
position of fire chief and provides a salary for the fire
chief, which position is presently filled by the supervisor-elect.
The district has not chosen to establish a civil service system
for its employees. Thus, it appears that the fire chief is an
"at-will" employee of the District.

Analysis of Question 1.
_____________________

Do the positions of fire chief of a county fire
protection district and member of a county board
of supervisors constitute incompatible offices?

I have found no constitutional provision or statute which expressly
permits or prohibits simultaneously holding the positions of dis-
trict fire chief and member of the county board of supervisors.
However, the common law provides that if both of these positions
constitute public offices and the offices are found to be ". . .
inconsistent, incompatible, or conflictive. . ." then they may not
be held by the same person at the same time. A general summary of
the law of incompatability of offices is as follows:


1/Commencing in November, 1986, the [illegible] will [illegible]
   be appointed by the Solano County [illegible] [illegible]
   will become an elective board.

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 3
------

"Two offices are said to be incompatible when the
holder cannot in every instance discharge the duties of
each. Incompatibility arises, therefore, from the nature
of the duties of the offices, when there is an irreconcil-
iency in the functions of the two, where the functions
of the two are inherently inconsistent or repugnant, as
where antagonism would result in the attempt by one
person to discharge the duties of both offices, or
where the nature and duties of the two offices are such
as to render it improper from considering of public
policy for one person to retain both. . . ." People ex
rel. Chapman v. Rapsey 16 Cal.2d 631, 641, 647 (15:40).
(Emphasis added.)

The public is entitled to have the full undivided
services of each public officer. . . . When these services
in the form of duties clash, divided loyalty is the
result.

The policy as stated in Chapman comprehends prospec-
tive as well as present clashes of loyalty. . . . Only
one significant clash of duties and loyalties is required
to make. . . . offices incompatible. . . . The existence of
devices to avoid . . . [conflicts] neither changes the
nature of the potential conflict nor provides assurance
that they would be employed. . . ." 56 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen.
488, 489.

The first factor to be determined is whether a member of the board
of supervisors and district fire chief are both public offices to
which the secondary issue of incompatibility would apply. See 40
Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 238, 239.

A member of a board of supervisors clearly holds a public office.
Government Code §24000(c); Coulter v. Fund [illegible] [illegible],
186, 187.

As for district fire chief, there is no question of the nature
of the position as a public office. A person who works for government--be
it as a city or county fire chief--holds a public office in the true sense of a
public benefit. In fact the term, "public office" in many ways and in so many senses that the courts have been unable
to precisely define the term adequately. . . . Absent an express constitutional
See Bennett v. Superior Court (1955) 131 CA2d 941 at 943. In the
absence of an express constitutional or statutory limitation of
public office, the courts have generally proceeded to elements

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 4

essential to the distinction between a public office and a mere
public employment: (1) stable tenure in the position and (2)
authority to exercise some of the sovereign rights of the public
entity.

With respect to the first issue--tenure--the point is that the
person's position should not be transient, occasional, or incidental; rather, the person should be vested with the position for
some fixed term or subject to removal only for cause, etc. In the
instant case, the district fire chief serves under contract with
the board of directors, has no set period of tenure, and the
position is "at will."

The second factor--exercise of sovereign rights--is also missing in
that those are the province of the board of directors of the fire
districts. The board of directors has the authority to direct the
efforts of the district from a policy standpoint, review and
approve the district budget, and direct the activities of the
employees of the district. The position of fire chief is subservient to the board of directors and is not vested with any authority to act on behalf of the district, other than those powers
expressly delegated to the fire chief by the board of directors.

In conclusion, it is my opinion that the position of Fire Chief of
Elmira Fire Protection District is an "employment" as opposed to
an "office." This, the position of fire chief and county supervisor
are not incompatible offices requiring disqualification under this
rule of law.

Analysis of Question 2.

From the perspective of the county board of supervisors, does the position of fire chief present a
conflict of interest arising from employment as
opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of
an office?

Several laws, including Section 4.7 of Division 4 of Title 1 of
the Government Code preclude incompatible activities in local
government when the conflict arises from an individual's employment.

Analysis of this issue relates to the question of whether there
would be times when, while serving in one capacity, one would be
precluded from acting in the other position. In the instant case,
the question would have to be answered in the affirmative. Several

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 5

examples are easy to envision: 1) When the Board of Supervisors
appoints a new member of the Board of Directors; 2) the fire dis-
trict pursuant to Health and Safety Code §13191(b); 3) if the
Board of Supervisors were required to confer a reorganization of
the fire district pursuant to Government Code §56199; 4) whenever
the Board of Supervisors distributed funds to the district in the
special district augmentation fund; or 5) whenever the responsibilitis
associated with attending Board of Supervisor meetings and committee
assignments conflicted with both emergency and non-emergency
duties with the fire district.

In general, there are four (4) conflict of interest provisions
which are at play in this case.

First is the contractual prohibition found in Government Code
§1090 et seq. This section forbids a governmental entity, and the
voting members thereof, from entering into a contract in which a
member is interested. Except in those situations in which the
interest is remote (G.C. §1090, §1092). Should a contract be
entered into between the governmental body and the other business
entity, the contract is void and may subject the parties to the
contract to removal from office. The conflicting contract section
does not appear to be at play in this instance since (1) there are
no contracts as contemplated within the section between the County
of Solano and the Elmira Fire Protection District, and (2) it
appears that the prohibition contained in the contract conflicts
statute was not intended to relate to contracts between governmental entities.

Second, under the California Political Reform Act (G.C. §87100 et
seq.), a public official may not make, participate in, or in any
way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest (G.C.
§7100.) Under Government Code §83103(a), financial interest
includes decisions where there would be a material effect or net
source of income to the public official when that source of income
would, during the course of a year, exceed $250.00. While the
section does suggest that a potential conflict of interest could
arise when activities are undertaken by the Board of Supervisors
on behalf of the Elmira Fire Protection District, the simple
answer is that a supervisor may refrain from participation in the
decision and, having done so, will have met the disclosure and
abstention requirements of the Political Reform Act. The Political
Reform Act predicates conflicts of interest upon a transactional
basis which permits a public official to disqualify himself when
conflicting situations arise. This analysis was found to be
appropriate in 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 916, wherein the California

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 6
------


Attorney General held that it would not be a conflict of interest
for a county supervisor within a particular air basin pollution
control district to contract with the district to provide air
pollution consulting services if the supervisor did not participate
in any transaction or decision of the board of supervisors relating
to air pollution.2/

Third, pursuant to Government Code §1125, the California Legislature
provided that officers and employees of a local agency shall not
engage in any employment or other activities for compensation
which are ". . . inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or
inimical. . . to their official duties." While the following
quote is rather long, it does summarize the law concerning the
applicability of the conflict section contained in Government Code
§1125 et seq:

"Though §§1125 et seq. were obviously intended pri-
marily for subordinate officers and employees of a local


2/
However, the opinion questions the supervisor's abdication of his responsibilities
of office due to the conflict, when analyzing with respect to Government
Code §1126. The opinion states:

"The supervisor in question has already agreed that he will
abstain from participation in any matter coming before the board
of supervisors with respect to air pollution control matters. How-
ever, as noted, section 1125 does not permit a local agency officer
to abdicate his responsibilities to that agency in favor of his
outside activities. The agreement to abstain from participation in
all air pollution matters which would come before the board of
supervisors would amount to just that, an abdication of his duties
to the district, an entity separate from the county. Whether such
abstention would in fact be required as to all or a major portion
of those duties we cannot say. This would require an analysis of
the duties actually assigned to the individual pursuant to the
basin contract contrasted with his duties as a district board member.
However, his agreement to abstain would indicate to us that there
would be tremendous difficulty in attempting to walk a fine line
perform the duties of both positions. Pertinent at [illegible]
section 1126 is the exception, not the [illegible]

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 7
------

agency, [the Attorney General] has held that Section 1126(a)
is self-executing3/ and is broad enough to encompass the
governing body of the local agency. We have additionally
held it to be applicable to outside public employment for
compensation. For example, . . . (the Attorney General
held that being a city firefighter would be incompatible
with holding the office of city councilman of the same
city. In this regard we applied by analogy the common law
doctrine concerning incompatible offices, and determined
that such analogy required that both positions could not
be held concurrently, and that the individual would have to
resign one of them. In short, it was determined that appro-
priate abstention could not avoid the incompatibility pro-
scription of §1126(a), just as abstention under the common
law doctrine relating to incompatible offices does not
avoid the doctrine. However, . . . incompatibility which
would give rise to the necessity to resign one position
would have to be such as would necessarily inure in the
two positions; that the possibility of an isolated trans-
actional conflict between two positions would not fall
within §1126(a).

Upon further reflection on §§ 1126 et seq., when
considered in relation to Proposition 9 and §§ 1090 et
seq. concerning contractual conflicts of interest, it
is the view of this office that our prior approach re-
quires modification. It is the current belief of this
office that the analogy between §§ 1125 et seq. of the
Government Code and the common law doctrine concerning
incompatible offices cannot be fully applied so as to
require resignation where incompatibility may inhere in
some of the functions of the two positions. This is
brought into relief when one considers that §§ 1126
(Political Reform Act) prohibits conflicts on a transactional
basis, and hence abstention is permitted as well as required.

Additionally, §§ 1090 et seq. permit resort on a
transactional basis where the conflict is defined statu-
torily as a 'remote interest.' Use Section 1091. Were
a strict application of the doctrine concerning incom-
patibility of offices to be applied by analogy, it con

37
Recently the courts have held that the section is not self-executing. See
Mazzola v. City and County of San Francisco 112 CAM 141. The court's
analysis in Mazzola would not apply to conflicts in the present case from
the county's standpoint but would if the fire district were to consider that
election to the board of supervisors conflicted with the duties of fire chief.

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 8

incompatibility found under §1125 as to private employment or other private activities, the anomalous situation could arise where the PRA would permit abstention, §1090 et seq. would permit abstention, and the general and somewhat obscure provisions §1125 et seq. would require resignation from one of the conflicting employments or activities. Such a result would render the operation of the PRA and §1090 et seq. meaningless in many cases. Such a result is to be avoided.

It is therefore concluded that §1125 et seq. does not require a resignation of one office or employment if an incompatibility is found within the meaning of §1126, but that abstention will be permitted on a transitional basis. The more specific provisions of the PRA and §§ 1090 et seq. should control over the more general provisions of §1125 insofar as they are covered by the former sections. We do not mean to hold, however, that if the incompatibility is of such a continuing and pervasive nature that a public officer or employee may constantly abstain from performing his duties because of personal conflict. In such a situation, resignation from the public office or employment or cessation of the conflicting private activity would appear to be required. 59 Ops.Cal.Atty. Gen. at 612-613 (Citations omitted).

Thus, in the context of §§ 1125 et seq., as to non-contractual matters, it would have to be resolved whether conflicts were of sufficient infrequency and relatively minor importance when tested against the sum total of the duties of the board. . . . So as to permit abstention, or whether the conflicts were of such a continuing and pervasive nature so as to render the holder of the office of board member ineffectual because of his inability to participate in board action. . . .

Under the Attorney General's analysis, it appears that the circumstances under which the Board of Supervisors would deal with the Intra Fire Protection District (appointment to the Board, organizations of the District, special district augmentation funds, etc.) would not constitute such instances of incompatibility of a ". . . continuing and pervasive nature. . ." that would require a constant abstention from the duties of either fire chief or Board of supervisor member due to the personal conflict.

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 9
------

Fourth, the courts of the State of California have generally
determined their conflict of interest decisions upon either a
statutory restriction or common law principles based upon sound
public policy against conflicts of interest. The courts have
consistently held that a conflict of interest may be declared when
the doing of the official act may place a public official in a
situation of having a direct or indirect interest in the outcome.
At the same time, the courts of the state have held that an interest
may be so remote and speculative so as not to create any conflict
of interest, although some cases suggest that any interest that
might affect an official's conduct creates a conflict. (See
Peo. v. Darby 114 CA2d 412, 435; see also Noble v. City of Palo
Alto 89 CA 47, 51; and Terry v. Bender 145 CA2d 198, 206. See
also 59 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., 604, 613-614.) Thus, the court's
analysis under common law principles is similar to that under
Government Code §1125 et seq.

Analysis of Question 3

From the perspective of the fire protection district,
does the position of fire chief and county supervisor
present a conflict of interest arising from employment
as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of
an officer?

From the perspective of the Board of Directors of the Rimira Fire
Protection District, the question of the election of their fire
chief to the County Board of Supervisors presents a different
legal analysis than that applicable to the conflict of interest
considerations arising from the election of the individual to the
Board of Supervisors as discussed above.

Under Government Code Section 1126(a) "A local agency . . . employed
shall not engage in any . . . activity . . . for compensation which
is inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to
the duties as a local agency . . . employee or with the proper
functions, or responsibilities of his appointing officer or the
agency by which he is employed." Were this section to apply, it
would appear that the position of fire chief would be with
the prohibition of Government Code Section 1126(a) and termination
loss of position as Fire Chief should be ordered. However, in
the case of Mazzie v. City and County of San Francisco, (1980) 112 Cal.App.3d [illegible] has held that Code
Section 1126(a) is not self-executing and the [illegible]
[illegible] (in most circumstances) by the District activity and appeal of [illegible]

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 10

employment or other disciplinary activity. The Court of Appeal
summarized their decision in the following paragraph:

"We think the intent of the legislature is clear.
Before a local agency applies this section in charging
an employee with its violation, that agency must prior
notice to the employee that a conflict of interest exists
with respect to the employee's outside activities. Notice
should also be provided to the employee with regard to the
agency's intended disciplinary action, as well as provisions
for appeal from the agency's determination. Where an agency
has not provided for notice of proscribed activities,
employees can only speculate whether or not a particular
activity they are engaging in will be prohibited. Clearly,
in the absence of any agency rules providing notice of
proscribed activities, a public employee or official who
decides to undertake an outside job might be subject at
any time and without warning to being charged with a violation of Section 1126."

With respect to the particular issue at hand, the following quotation
from the Mazzola case, is instructive:

"We do not suggest that whenever an appointment to
a public office is made with full knowledge of an appointee's outside employment, the appointing officer or public
agency is thereafter estopped from ever bringing charge
against the appointee based on a violation of Section 1126.
For example, where an official's outside or agency position
has changed, a subsequent finding of a conflict of interest
might be appropriate.

In such a case, however, it would contravene the
intended application of the statute to allow an agency to
file charges based on its redetermination of an employee's
status without first informing the employee of such a
change. By an agency's providing adequate notice of such
a redetermination to the employee, as provided in the last
paragraph of Section 1126, the employee would have a chance
to either appeal the agency's determination or to refrain from
either his outside or agency position. However, without
such notice, an employee would be forever subject to being
charged outright with an 1126 violation, even after his
outside activity has previously been approved." Mazzola;
p.154.

The Board of Directors of the Elmira City School District concurred
with the situation in which a change in circumstance had occurred

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 11
-------

with respect to their Fire Chief. I do not believe the case falls
within the Mazzoia rationale but, rather, is one of a changing
circumstances which now provides the Board of Directors with an
opportunity to determine whether or not the outside activities of
their Fire Chief, with respect to his duties as a Solano County
Supervisor warrants some action.

In my opinion, the Board of Directors may determine whether the
outside activities as a Board of Supervisor member will diminish
or decrease the ability of the Fire Chief to function as such, and
to perform the duties of his employment as expected by the Board
of Directors. If that is found to be the case, the Board of
Directors may determine either to dismiss the Fire Chief from his
position or otherwise place conditions upon his continued employment
in order to integrate the two functions if, in their judgment,
this is possible. Any such determination by the Board of Directors
should be made after giving notice of any proposed determination
an opportunity to be heard by the Fire Chief on the proposed
action.

Analysis of Question 4

If the district fire chief were to waive his salary
and act in a volunteer capacity as fire chief (or
district firefighter) would the analyses be different
from that posed in question (3) above?

In my view, a County Supervisor may serve as a volunteer fireman
and, in that capacity as a volunteer fireman, may hold a variety
of leadership positions within the fire department, including
volunteer fire chief if in fact that is the organizational structure which the Board of Directors wishes to create for purposes of
serving the District's fire protection needs.

However, it is also my opinion that the Board of Directors of the
Fire Protection District still may determine whether they wish to
utilize this mechanism or not since it is apparent that the duties
of a full-time fire chief (volunteer or paid) will, in all probability, conflict at some time in the future with the activities as
a Board of Supervisor member either at the regular Tuesday meeting
of the Board of Supervisors or at other meetings of such or subcommittees which the Supervisor may be required to attend.
After analysis, it may well be the decision of the Board to seek
that the full-time services of the Fire Chief, as a fire
fighter for that matter, must be paramount as a primary
activity, and that the issue of [illegible]
determination.

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 12

Analysis of Question 5

Is any conflict avoided by the district board becoming
an elective body?

As noted in footnote 1, the fire district board will become an
elective body in November, 1983. While this will render the
board, for all intents and purposes, an independent body of the
board of supervisors and will eliminate any question of the appointment of individuals to the board of directors, the question of
conflicts of interests are not resolved by this event. As noted
earlier in the opinion request, several possible conflicts were
mentioned and, of those, only the one concerning the appointment
of the board of directors would be resolved by having the board
become an independently elected body. The board of supervisors
would still be involved in a district reorganization, distribute
district augmentation funds and the situation might give rise to a
time conflict between the two positions.

In my opinion, the change from an appointive board to an elective
board would not make a fundamental change in the analysis. From
the perspective of the County Board of Supervisors, the analysis
will remain that the position is one of public office and public
employment, and that in certain circumstances, the supervisor
would be obligated to abstain from board of supervisors action, but
the two positions would not result in a circumstance giving rise
to the loss of one position or the other.

With respect to the Board of Directors of the Fire Protection District, the analysis would likewise remain the same in that the
outside activity represented by the election to the County Board
of Supervisors would give rise to the consideration of a conflict
as to the function and duties of the fire chief of the district.
As noted in the analysis to question 3 above and under the Mazzia
case, it is up to the Board of Directors to determine whether or
not the provisions of Government Code §1126 would apply and, if
so, to provide notice and opportunity to be heard to the fire
chief should the board determination would adversely affect the
individual's employment as a fire chief.

CONCLUSION

Clearly the supervisor-elect "creates the appearance of a conflict" by holding the position of Fire Chief of the Clima Fire

Honorable J. Winkler
Page 13

Protection District; however, circumstances requiring abstention
of the supervisor-elect are not pervasive because of the limited
interaction between the county and the district. Criticism in the
future--justified or not-must be expected whenever abstention is
necessary or where someone perceives that lack of attention to
their problem (whether county-related or arising from the duties
of a fire chief) is the result of efforts directed to the other
responsibilities. Caution must be the keynote when dealing with
conflicts of interest even though the ability of the supervisor to
deal with the Elmira Fire Protection District will be curtailed,
it is my opinion that those instances do not appear to be so
substantially as to negate the supervisor's ability to handle the
vast majority of county business.

Respectfully submitted,

[Signature]
CHARLES O. LAMORPE
County Counsel

COL:eb

sections.conclusion:
Clearly the supervisor-elect "creates the appearance of a conflict" by holding the position of Fire Chief of the Elmira Fire

Honorable J. Winkler

Protection District; however, circumstances requiring abstention of the supervisor-elect are not pervasive because of the limited interaction between the county and the district. Criticism in the future—justified or not—must be expected whenever abstention is necessary or where someone perceives that lack of attention to their problem (whether county-related or arising from the duties of a fire chief) is the result of efforts directed to the other responsibilities. Caution must be the keynote when dealing with conflicts of interest even though the ability of the supervisor to deal with the Elmira Fire Protection District will be curtailed, it is my opinion that those instances do not appear to be so substantial as to negate the supervisor's ability to handle the vast majority of county business.
sections.analysis:
of Question 1.

Do the positions of fire chief of a county fire protection district and member of a county board of supervisors constitute incompatible offices?

I have found no constitutional provision or statute which expressly permits or prohibits simultaneously holding the positions of district fire chief and member of the county board of supervisors. However, the common law provides that if both of these positions constitute public offices and the offices are found to be "... inconsistent, incompatible, or conflictive..." then they may not be held by the same person at the same time. A general summary of the law of incompatibility of offices is as follows:

1/ Commencing in November, 1983, the District Board of Directors will be appointed by the Solano County Board of Supervisors and will become an elective board.

Honorable J. Winkler

"Two offices are said to be incompatible when the holder cannot in every instance discharge the duties of each. Incompatibility arises, therefore, from the nature of the duties of the offices, when there is an inconsistency in the functions of the two, where the functions of the two are inherently inconsistent or repugnant, as where antagonism would result in the attempt by one person to discharge the duties of both offices, or where the nature and duties of the two offices are such as to render it improper from considerations of public policy for one person to retain both..." People ex rel. Chapman v. Rapsey 16 Cal.2d 630, 641, 642 (1940). (Emphasis added.)

The public is entitled to have the full undivided services of each public officer... When these services in the form of duties clash, divided loyalty is the result.

The policy as stated in Chapman comprehends prospective as well as present clashes of loyalty. "... Only one significant clash of duties and loyalties is required to make... offices incompatible... The existence of devices to avoid... [conflicts] neither changes the nature of the potential conflict nor provides assurance that they would be employed..." 56 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen, 488, 489.

The first factor to be determined is whether a member of the board of supervisors and district fire chief are both public offices to which the secondary issue of incompatability would apply. See 40 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 238, 239.

A member of a board of supervisors clearly holds a "public office." Government Code §24000(c); Coulter v. Pool (1951) 107 Cal.App. 185, 187.

As for district fire chief, there is no express statutory declaration of the position as a public office. To be sure, every person who works for government--be it county supervisor or fire chief--holds a public office in the sense of one employed for the public benefit. In fact the term, "public office" is used in so many ways and in so many senses that the courts have been unable to precisely define the term adequately to cover every situation. See Bennett v. Superior Court (1955) 131 CA2d 841 at 843. In the absence of an express constitutional or statutory creation of a public office, the courts have generally regarded two elements as

essential to the distinction between a public office and a mere public employment: (1) stable tenure in the position and (2) authority to exercise some of the sovereign rights of the public entity.

With respect to the first issue--tenure--the point is that the person's position should not be transient, occasional, or incidental; rather, the person should be vested with the position for some fixed term or subject to removal only for cause, etc. In the instant case, the district fire chief serves under contract with the board of directors, has no set period of tenure, and the position is "at will."

The second factor--exercise of sovereign rights--is also missing in that those are the province of the board of directors of the fire districts. The board of directors has the authority to direct the efforts of the district from a policy standpoint, review and approve the district budget, and direct the activities of the employees of the district. The position of fire chief is subservient to the board of directors and is not vested with any authority to act on behalf of the district, other than those powers expressly delegated to the fire chief by the board of directors.

In conclusion, it is my opinion that the position of Fire Chief of Elmira Fire Protection District is an "employment" as opposed to an "office." Thus, the position of fire chief and county supervisor are not incompatible offices requiring disqualification under this rule of law.

Analysis of Question 2.

From the perspective of the county board of supervisors, does the position of fire chief present a conflict of interest arising from employment as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of an office?

Several laws, including Section 4.7 of Division 4 of Title I of the Government Code preclude incompatible activities in local government when the conflict arises from an individual's employment.

Analysis of this issue relates to the question of whether there would be times when, while serving in one capacity, one would be precluded from acting in the other position. In the instant case, the question would have to be answered in the affirmative. Several

examples are easy to envision: 1) When the Board of Supervisors appoints a new member of the board of directors of the fire district, pursuant to Health and Safety Code §13831(b); 2) If the Board of Supervisors were required to consider a reorganization of the fire district pursuant to Government Code §56195; 3) whenever the Board of Supervisors distributed funds to the district via the special district augmentation fund; or 4) whenever the responsibilities associated with attending Board of Supervisor meetings and committee assignments conflicted with both emergency and non-emergency duties with the fire district.

In general, there are four (4) conflict of interest provisions which are at play in this case.

First is the contractual prohibition found in Government Code §1090 et seq. This section forbids a governmental entity, and the voting members thereof, from entering into a contract in which a member is interested except in those situations in which the interest is remote (G.C. §1090, §1092). Should a contract be entered into between the governmental body and the other business or entity, the contract is void and may subject the parties to the contract to removal from office. The conflicting contract section does not appear to be at play in this instance since (1) there are no contracts as contemplated within the section between the County of Solano and the Elmira Fire Protection District, and (2) it appears that the prohibition contained in the contract conflicts statute was not intended to relate to contracts between governmental entities.

Second, under the California Political Reform Act (G.C. §87100 et seq.), a public official may not make, participate in, or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial interest. (G.C. §87100.) Under Government Code §83103(a), financial interest includes decisions where there would be a material effect on any source of income to the public official when that source of income would, during the course of a year, exceed $250.00. While this section does suggest that a potential conflict of interest could arise when activities are undertaken by the Board of Supervisors on behalf of the Elmire Fire Protection District, the simple answer is that a supervisor may refrain from participation in the decision and, having done so, will have met the disclosure and abstention requirements of the Political Reform Act. The Political Reform Act predicates conflicts of interest upon a transactional basis which permits a public official to disqualify himself when conflicting situations arise. This analysis was found to be appropriate in 63 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 916, wherein the California

Attorney General held that it would not be a conflict of interest for a county supervisor within a particular air basin air pollution control district to contract with the district to provide air pollution consulting services if the supervisor did not participate in any transaction or decision of the board of supervisors relating to air pollution.2/

Third, pursuant to Government Code §1125, the California Legislature provided that officers and employees of a local agency shall not engage in any employment or other activities for compensation which are ". . . inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical. . . to their official duties." While the following quote is rather long, it does summarize the law concerning the applicability of the conflict section contained in Government Code §1125 et seq:

"Though §§1125 et seq. were obviously intended primarily for subordinate officers and employees of a local

2/
However, the opinion questions the supervisor's abdication of the responsibilities of office due to the conflict, when analyzed with respect to Government Code §1126. The opinion states:

"The supervisor in question has already agreed that he will abstain from participation in any matter coming before the board of supervisors with respect to air pollution control matters. However, as noted, section 1126 does not permit a local agency officer to abdicate his responsibilities to that agency in favor of his outside activities. The agreement to abstain from participation in all air pollution matters which would come before the board of supervisors would amount to just that, an abdication of his duties to the district, an entity separate from the county. Whether such abstention would in fact be required as to all or a major portion of those duties we cannot say. This would require an analysis of the duties actually assigned to the individual pursuant to the air basin contract contrasted with his duties as a district board member. However, his agreement to abstain would indicate to us that there would be tremendous difficulty in attempting to walk a 'fine line' and perform the duties of both positions. Permitted abdication under section 1126 is the exception, not the rule."

Honorable J. Winkler

agency, [the Attorney General] has held that Section 1126(a) is self-executing3/ and is broad enough to encompass the governing body of the local agency. We have additionally held it to be applicable to outside public employment for compensation. For example, . . . (the Attorney General held that being a city firefighter would be incompatible with holding the office of city councilman of the same city. In this regard we applied by analogy the common law doctrine concerning incompatible offices, and determined that such analogy required that both positions could not be held concurrently, and that the individual would have to resign one of them. In short, it was determined that appropriate abstention could not avoid the incompatibility proscription of §1126(a), just as abstention under the common law doctrine relating to incompatible offices does not avoid the doctrine. However, . . . incompatibility which would give rise to the necessity to resign one position would have to be such as would necessarily inhere in the two positions; that the possibility of an isolated transactional conflict between two positions would not fall within §1126(a).

Upon further reflection on §§ 1126 et seq., when considered in relation to Proposition 9 and §§ 1090 et seq. concerning contractual conflicts of interest, it is the view of this office that our prior approach requires modification. It is the current belief of this office that the analogy between §§ 1125 et seq. of the Government Code and the common law doctrine concerning incompatible offices cannot be fully applied so as to require resignation where incompatibility may inhere in some of the functions of the two positions. This is brought into relief when one considers that the PRA (Political Reform Act) prohibits conflicts on a transactional basis, and hence abstention is permitted as well as required.

Additionally, §§ 1090 et seq. permit abstention on a transactional basis where the conflict is defined statutorily as a 'remote interest.' See Section 1091. Were a strict application of the doctrine concerning incompatibility of offices to be applied by analogy to an

3/
Recently the courts have held that the section is not self-executing. See Mazzola v. City and County of San Francisco 112 CA3d 141. The court's analysis in Mazzola would not apply to conflicts in the present case from the county's standpoint but would if the fire district were to consider that election to the board of supervisors conflicted with the duties of fire chief.

incompatibility found under §1125 as to private employment or other private activities, the anomalous situation could arise where the PRA would permit abstention, §1090 et seq. would permit abstention, and the general and somewhat obscure provisions §1125 et seq. would require resignation from one of the conflicting employments or activities. Such a result would render the operation of the PRA and §1090 et seq. meaningless in many cases. Such a result is to be avoided.

It is therefore concluded that §1125 et seq. does not require a resignation of one office or employment if an incompatibility is found within the meaning of §1126, but that abstention will be permitted on a transactional basis. The more specific provisions of the PRA and §§ 1090 et seq. should control over the more general provisions of 1125 insofar as they are covered by the former sections. We do not mean to hold, however, that if the incompatibility is of such a continuing and pervasive nature that a public officer or employee may constantly abstain from performing his duties because of personal conflict. In such a situation, resignation from the public office or employment or cessation of the conflicting private activity would appear to be required. 59 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. at 612-613 (Citations omitted).

Thus, in the context of §§ 1125 et seq., as to non-contractual matters... it would have to be resolved whether conflicts were of sufficient infrequency and relatively minor importance when tested against the sum total of the duties of the board... so as to permit abstention, or whether the conflicts were of such a continuing and pervasive nature so as to render the holder of the office of board member ineffectual because of his inability to participate in board action....

Under the Attorney General's analysis, it appears that the circumstances under which the Board of Supervisors would deal with the Elmira Fire Protection District (appointments to the Board, reorganizations of the District, special district augmentation funds, etc.) would not constitute such instances of incompatibility of a "... continuing and pervasive nature..." that would require a constant abstention from the duties of either fire chief or board of supervisor member due to the personal conflict.

Fourth, the courts of the State of California have generally determined their conflict of interest decisions upon either a statutory restriction or common law principles based upon sound public policy against conflicts of interest. The courts have consistently held that a conflict of interest may be declared when the doing of the official act may place a public official in a situation of having a direct or indirect interest in the outcome. At the same time, the courts of the state have held that an interest may be so remote and speculative so as not to create any conflict of interest, although some cases suggest that any interest that might affect an official's conduct creates a conflict. (See Peo. v. Darby 114 CA2d 412, 435; see also Noble v. City of Palo Alto 89 CA 47, 51; and Terry v. Bender 145 CA2d 198, 206. See also 59 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen., 604, 613-614.) Thus, the court's analysis under common law principles is similar to that under Government Code §1125 et seq.

Analysis of Question 3.

From the perspective of the fire protection district, does the position of fire chief and county supervisor present a conflict of interest arising from employment as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of an office?

From the perspective of the Board of Directors of the Elmira Fire Protection District, the question of the election of their fire chief to the County Board of Supervisors presents a different legal analysis than that applicable to the conflict of interest considerations arising from the election of the individual to the Board of Supervisors as discussed above.

Under Government Code Section 1126(a) "A local agency... employee shall not engage in any... activity... for compensation which is inconsistent, incompatible, in conflict with, or inimical to his duties as a local agency... employee or with the duties, functions, or responsibilities of his appointing power or the agency by which he is employed." Were this section self-executing, it would appear that the position of Fire Chief would come within the prohibition of Government Code Section 1126(a) and result in loss of position as Fire Chief should the office of County Supervisor be accepted. However, the case of Mazzola v. City and County of San Francisco, (1980) 112 Cal.App.3d 147, has held that Government Code Section 1126(a) is not self-executing, rather, it must be preceded (in most circumstances) by notice of the inconsistent activity and appeal of the determination.

Honorable J. Winkler

employment or other disciplinary activity. The Court of Appeal summarized their decision in the following paragraph:

"We think the intent of the legislature is clear. Before a local agency applies this section in charging an employee with its violation, that agency must provide notice to the employee that a conflict of interest exists with respect to the employee's outside activities. Notice should also be provided to the employee with regard to the agency's intended disciplinary action, as well as provisions for appeal from the agency's determination. Where an agency has not provided for notice of proscribed activities, employees can only speculate whether or not a particular activity they are engaging in will be prohibited. Clearly, in the absence of any agency rules providing notice of proscribed activities, a public employee or official who decides to undertake an outside job might be subject at any time and without warning to being charged with a violation of Section 1126."

With respect to the particular issue at hand, the following quotation from the Mazzola case, is instructive:

"We do not suggest that whenever an appointment to a public office is made with full knowledge of an appointee's outside employment, the appointing officer or public agency is thereafter estopped from ever bringing charges against the appointee based on a violation of Section 1126. For example, where an official's outside or agency position has changed, a subsequent finding of a conflict of interest might be appropriate.

In such a case, however, it would contravene the intended application of the statute to allow an agency to file charges based on its redetermination of an employee's status without first informing the employee of such a change. By an agency's providing adequate notice of such a redetermination to the employee, as provided in the last paragraph of Section 1126, the employee would have a chance to either appeal the agency's determination or resign from either his outside or agency position. However, without such notice, an employee would be forever subject to being charged outright with an 1126 violation, even after his outside activity has previously been approved." Mazzola; p.154.

The Board of Directors of the Elmira Fire District are confronted with the situation in which a change in circumstances has occurred

with respect to their Fire Chief. I do not believe the case falls within the Mazzola rationale but, rather, is one of a changing circumstances which now provides the Board of Directors with an opportunity to determine whether or not the outside activities of their Fire Chief, with respect to his duties as a Solano County Supervisor warrants some action.

In my opinion, the Board of Directors may determine whether the outside activities as a Board of Supervisor member will diminish or decrease the ability of the Fire Chief to function as such, and to perform the duties of his employment as expected by the Board of Directors. If that is found to be the case, the Board of Directors may determine either to dismiss the Fire Chief from his position or otherwise place conditions upon his continued employment in order to integrate the two functions if, in their judgment, this is possible. Any such determination by the Board of Directors should be made after giving notice of any proposed determination an opportunity to be heard by the Fire Chief on the proposed action.

Analysis of Question 4

If the district fire chief were to waive his salary and act in a volunteer capacity as fire chief (or district firefighter) would the analyses be different from that posed in question (3) above?

In my view, a County Supervisor may serve as a volunteer fireman and, in that capacity as a volunteer fireman, may hold a variety of leadership positions within the fire department, including volunteer fire chief if in fact that is the organizational structure which the Board of Directors wishes to create for purposes of serving the District's fire protection needs.

However, it is also my opinion that the Board of Directors of the Fire Protection District still may determine whether they wish to utilize this mechanism or not since it is apparent that the duties of a full-time fire chief (volunteer or paid) will, in all probability, conflict at some time in the future with the activities as a Board of Supervisor member either at the regular Tuesday meetings of the Board of Supervisors or at other meetings of commissions or subcommittees which the Supervisor may be required to attend. After analysis, it may well be the decision of the Board of Directors that the full-time services of the Fire Chief, or of any firefighter for that matter, must be paramount to any other outside activity, and that the issue of payment must be left to that determination.

Analysis of Question 5

Is any conflict avoided by the district board becoming an elective body?

As noted in footnote 1, the fire district board will become an elective body in November, 1983. While this will render the board, for all intents and purposes, an independent body of the board of supervisors and will eliminate any question of the appointment of individuals to the board of directors, the questions of conflicts of interests are not resolved by this event. As noted earlier in the opinion request, several possible conflicts were mentioned and, of those, only the one concerning the appointment of the board of directors would be resolved by having the board become an independently elected body. The board of supervisors would still be involved in a district reorganization, distribute district augmentation funds and the situation might give rise to a time conflict between the two positions.

In my opinion, the change from an appointive board to an elective board would not make a fundamental change in the analysis. From the perspective of the County Board of Supervisors, the analysis will remain that the position is one of public office and public employment, and that in certain circumstances, the supervisor would be obligated to abstain from board of supervisor action, but the two positions would not result in a circumstance giving rise to the loss of one position or the other.

With respect to the Board of Directors of the Fire Protection District, the analysis would likewise remain the same in that the outside activity represented by the election to the County Board of Supervisors would give rise to the consideration of a conflict as to the function and duties of the fire chief of the district. As noted in the analysis to question 3 above and under the Mazzola case, it is up to the Board of Directors to determine whether or not the provisions of Government Code §1126 would apply and, if so, to provide notice and opportunity to be heard to the fire chief should the board determination would adversely affect the individual's employment as a fire chief.

embedding.qa_text:
CONCLUSION: Clearly the supervisor-elect "creates the appearance of a conflict" by holding the position of Fire Chief of the Elmira Fire

Honorable J. Winkler

Protection District; however, circumstances requiring abstention of the supervisor-elect are not pervasive because of the limited interaction between the county and the district. Criticism in the future—justified or not—must be expected whenever abstention is necessary or where someone perceives that lack of attention to their problem (whether county-related or arising from the duties of a fire chief) is the result of efforts directed to the other responsibilities. Caution must be the keynote when dealing with conflicts of interest even though the ability of the supervisor to deal with the Elmira Fire Protection District will be curtailed, it is my opinion that those instances do not appear to be so substantial as to negate the supervisor's ability to handle the vast majority of county business.
embedding.first_500_words: March 16, 1983 Clayton Roche Deputy Attorney General 350 McAllister St., Room 6000 San Francisco, CA 94102 Re: Your Opinion No. 82-1207 Our No. A-83-071 Dear Mr. Roche: Thank you for forwarding Mr. Lamoree's opinion request to this office. Most of the matters covered by the opinion request are outside the scope of the Political Reform Act. However, I would like to point out, in connection with Mr. Lamoree's analysis of the Political Reform Act (pages 5-6 of his letter), that his salary as a fire chief is a salary from a local government agency and thus is not "income" within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Government Code Section 82030(b)(2). 1/ As a result, the fire district is not a "source of income" to him, and he would not have to disqualify himself, pursuant to Sections 87100 and 87103, from making decisions as a county supervisor which would affect the fire district or which would affect his own salary as an employee of the district. 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(d)(3) and (f)(2) (a copy of 18700 is included for your convenience). Thank you for giving us an opportunity to comment on this request. Very truly yours, Barbara A. Milman Chief Legal Division BAM:ms 1/ All statutory references made are to the Government Code unless otherwise stated. March 14, 1983 Barbara A. Milman Chief, Legal Division Fair Political Practices Commission P.O. Box 807 Sacramento, CA 95804 Dear Ms. Milman: Re: Opinion No. 82-1207 Enclosed is a copy of an opinion request which I have begun work on but had overlooked forwarding to your office at the time of receipt for views on the conflict of interest aspects of this dual office holding. Accordingly, any views you may wish to submit are hereby requested. Very truly yours, JOHN K. VAN DE KAMP Attorney General CLAYTON P. ROCHE Deputy Attorney General CPR:lv encls. The letter is from the Office of County Counsel in Solano County, California, addressed to Assistant Attorney General Jack R. Winkler. The county counsel seeks an opinion from the Attorney General regarding several questions about conflicts of interest involving positions of fire chief and county board of supervisors members. Here's a summary of the questions: 1. Do the positions of fire chief of a county fire protection district and member of a county board of supervisors constitute incompatible offices? 2. From the perspective of the county board of supervisors, does the position of fire chief present a conflict of interest arising from employment as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of an office? 3. From the perspective of the fire protection district, does the position of fire chief and county supervisor present a conflict of interest arising from employment as opposed to a conflict arising from the holding of an office? 4. If the district fire chief were to waive his salary and act in a volunteer capacity as fire chief (or district firefighter) would the analyses be different from that posed in question (3)
